lines.text: Homeroom still has that first-two-weeks smell — new notebooks, old air conditioning. Ms. Alvarez waits for the room to settle, which takes a while.
Two weeks from today, you'll present a team showcase. It's forty percent of your quarter grade.
lines.action: taps two fingers on the desk

lines.text: So — no pressure.
Okay. Some pressure.
A ripple of nervous laughter. She reads down the list of names without looking up.
Theo, Marcus, Priya — you're a team.
Nice. I got the funny one and the smart one.
I'm basically covered.
There are four required components.
lines.action: still reading the assignment sheet, not looking up

lines.text: Did anyone else catch that there are four required components?
Honestly? I caught 'group project' and kind of blacked out after that.
Same, honestly. Bold of her to expect us to read the whole page.
It's one page.
Before you scatter — I want each team to divide up roles. Out loud, so I hear it.
Theo, let's start with you.
Library study room. Priya already has a folder open and labeled. Marcus is leaning back so far his chair creaks.
Okay, hear me out.
My sister did basically this exact project last year. Got a ribbon at the regional fair and everything.
...Okay?
So we could just, like, use hers as a starting point. It's not even the same class. Nobody's gonna know.
Priya goes very still. Her eyes flick to the player before she says anything.
Marcus.
What? I'm just saying it's already good. Why reinvent the wheel.
Because it's not our wheel.
It's basically a suggestion. A jumping-off point.
So. What do we think?
Fine, fine. From scratch.
Love starting from literally nothing, two weeks before it's due. Great use of time.
I had an idea. It's probably stupid.
lines.action: opens her notebook, doesn't quite look up

lines.text: No, go — what've you got?
Okay, so — what if the base rotated?
lines.emotion: in a rush now that she's been asked

lines.text: Instead of a static display, the whole thing turns, so people can see every side without touching it.
Wait, hold on.
Like a lazy Susan. But for science.
...Essentially, yes.
Okay that's actually kind of sick.
See, this is already better than a hand-me-down ribbon.
Low blow.
It's slower than copying would've been. But for the first time, all three of them are leaning over the same notebook.
See, changing the title slide alone basically counts as original research.
I swapped the color scheme. And the font.
Look at us. Innovators.
Honestly? This feels—
lines.emotion: trailing off, glancing at the original file

lines.text: —fast. Really fast.
Fast is good! Fast means we get to go home before dinner.
Two days from a blank folder to a finished model does feel... unusually fast.
Right? We're basically geniuses.
Everyone's laughing. It looks great on the screen. Something small keeps tightening in the player's stomach anyway.
Marcus's section still says 'in progress.'
lines.action: refreshing the shared folder for what is clearly not the first time

lines.text: It has said 'in progress' since Tuesday.
Has he answered your texts?
Two check marks. No reply.
Marcus slides in late, doesn't quite make eye contact with either of them.
Hey — sorry, sorry, I'm here.
You're here. Is your part here.
It's fine, it's fine, I got it, I just—
lines.action: taps his pen against the table, stalling

lines.text: —I got it.
We don't have time for 'I got it.'
Priya catches the player alone in the hallway after the session, voice low.
Can I say something. Just to you.
...Yeah. Of course.
This is basically his sister's project with a new title slide.
Doesn't that feel wrong to you?
I already checked the folder. Twice. It says 'last edited two days ago' — from before we even touched it.
That's not a coincidence. That's a paper trail.
You've been sitting on this.
I didn't want to be the one who ruins it.
But it's getting harder to pretend I don't notice.
Hey — can we actually talk for a sec. Not the hallway version.
...About?
You keep saying you've got it. You don't got it. What's actually going on?
A long pause. Marcus taps his pen against the locker instead of answering right away.
...Yeah. Things have been kind of a lot at home.
I didn't— I didn't know how to say that without it turning into a whole thing.
It doesn't have to turn into a whole thing. I just need to know if you need help.
...Yeah. I think I do, actually.
Okay. So we figure it out. Together — not you disappearing and me guessing.
Honestly? Nobody's asked like that before.
The kitchen clock says 11:42. The half-finished model sits under a lamp that's too bright for this time of night.
This would take five minutes to explain to Marcus. It's taking me three hours to just... do it instead.
There. Done.
lines.action: sets the last piece down, quiet

lines.text: Nobody's gonna know I did this. That's kind of the point. And also kind of the problem.
The player checks their phone. No messages. Not from Marcus, not from Priya.
I'm fine. I'm great. I am a hundred percent fine and definitely didn't just do someone else's homework at midnight.
Marcus.
Finish your part. Then we'll talk about the part you skipped.
...Yes, ma'am.
Marcus, I didn't mean to—
It's fine.
I said I'd finish it. I'll finish it.
You did the right thing telling me.
That doesn't always feel like it in the moment.
...Doesn't feel like much of anything right now.
lines.action: watching Marcus leave without a word

lines.text: We need to tell you something.
...Okay, that's an ominous way to start a sentence.
We're not using your sister's project. We're starting over.
A long beat. Marcus doesn't argue right away.
Honestly? Kind of relieved. I felt weird every time I opened that folder.
You did?
Yeah. I just didn't know how to say it without sounding like I was killing the easy option.
Well. Consider it killed. Priya's got an idea, though.
It's probably stupid—
—okay, what if the base rotated—
Wait, I'm listening.
They work past dinner, past tired, into something that's actually, finally, theirs.
...Okay.
Great! Adapting is basically half the work anyway.
We just need to swap enough that it doesn't look—
—like it is what it is.
Understood.
Over the next few sessions, Priya stops sketching new ideas. She just executes.
Priya, you're quiet today.
I'm working.
The player notices. Says nothing. It gets easier the second time.
Priya said you didn't really... back her up. When she brought up the whole copying thing.
It wasn't really my call to make.
Huh. Okay.
Priya walks in. Polite. Cool.
Hi. Are we reviewing the slides or not.
Priya, I just meant—
It's fine.
lines.emotion: even, closing the door on it

lines.text: Let's just finish this.
It is, in fact, not fine. It just isn't going to be said out loud.
The rotating base spins smoothly under the classroom lights. A few classmates lean in, actually curious.
Nice work on the mechanism.
Who wants to walk me through the division of labor?
...I mean, Priya basically designed the whole rotation system, and Theo—
lines.action: taps his pen, unsure how much of the rough patch to mention

lines.text: —Theo kind of, held things together. When I wasn't.
The board looks great. Half the class is impressed. The other half is bored, which is honestly the goal.
This looks a lot like a project from the regional fair a couple years back.
lines.action: tilts her head, taps two fingers on her desk

lines.text: Where did the idea come from?
The room goes very quiet.

lines.action: stares at the desk, pen tapping a four-count under the table, saying nothing

lines.text: Thanks. For saying that in there.
I meant it.
Yeah. I know.
Sam catches up in the hallway, still holding a half-eaten granola bar.
Okay but did you guys actually finish the big project or is it still 'basically done' in the metaphysical sense.
...Thanks for not making it weird in there.
lines.action: not quite looking at the player

lines.text: Yeah. No problem.
Something unsaid sits between them anyway, small but present, like a shoe with a pebble in it.
Wait— you good? You've got the face.
The face you get.
A zero teaches you to hide better next time. A redo teaches you something else.
I'd rather teach you the second thing. You've got until Friday.
...Thank you.
lines.emotion: the first real breath she's taken in days

lines.text: Yeah. Thanks, Ms. Alvarez.
Sam catches up after the bell, unable to read a room to save their life, in the best way.
Lowkey I don't even care what grade you got, I just want to know if it was chaos. Was it chaos.
Ms. Alvarez holds the look a second too long, then moves on to the next group.

lines.action: won't meet the player's eyes, gathering the slides in silence

lines.text: Priya—
Nice presentation.
Sam catches up in the hallway, doing their best impression of someone who hasn't noticed anything's wrong.
Okay but did you guys actually finish it or is Marcus still 'working on his part' in the metaphysical sense—
—wait. You've got the face.

options.text: Team leader — I'll help keep us on track.
Researcher — I'll dig into the material.
Support role — I'll help wherever's needed.

a.text: Sure, go be in charge, boss.

b.text: Good. Someone should double-check the rubric before we build anything.

c.text: Support's clutch, honestly. That's the guy who saves the project at hour eleven.
We'll see who ends up doing that.

options.text: "That's basically cheating. Let's come up with our own idea."
"I mean... let's at least look at it for inspiration."
"Maybe we should ask Ms. Alvarez what's actually allowed first."

a.text: Okay, harsh, but— fine. Fine! We'll do it the hard way.

b.text: See, that's all I'm asking. Just a peek.
"Inspiration" is doing a lot of work in that sentence.

c.text: Ugh, buzzkill move. But — okay, fair.
That's actually the responsible thing to do.

options.text: Pull Marcus aside and ask what's actually going on.
Quietly do his part yourself so it gets done.
Tell Ms. Alvarez he's not pulling his weight.

a.text: ...That's probably smarter than what I was about to do.

b.text: You're just going to... do it? For him?
It's faster than arguing about it.

c.text: That feels like a last resort, not a first one.
Honestly? We're kind of out of resorts.

options.text: "You're right. Let's tell Marcus and start over, honestly."
"We've come too far now — let's just keep this between us."
"It was Marcus's idea, not mine — go talk to him."

a.emotion: quiet exhale, like she'd been holding her breath

a.text: Thank you for not making me say it twice.

c.text: It was his idea. You didn't say no.

options.text: Speak up and credit the whole team, including how Marcus turned things around.
Let Marcus answer for himself and stay out of it.

a.text: Honestly? Marcus was going through something none of us knew about, and once we talked, he showed up. All the way.
Good teamwork looks like that more often than people think.

b.emotion: letting it sit there a beat

options.text: Admit it was based too closely on an old project, and apologize.
Insist it's completely original.

a.text: It's based on Marcus's sister's old project. We changed some things, but... not enough. That's on us. Mostly me.
I appreciate you telling me that in front of the whole class. That took something.

b.text: No, it's — it's ours. We just, um, had similar ideas, I guess.

b.emotion: not buying it, but letting it go for now

options.text: "Honestly? Rough start, but we pulled together in the end."
"It went fine." (leave it there)

a.text: Okay wait, chaos details, please. I need the full timeline.

b.text: That's it? 'Fine'? You always say more than that.
b.emotion: genuinely curious, not letting it go

options.text: "Fine, but I keep wondering if I should've said more in there."
"It worked out. That's all that matters."

a.text: Lowkey, saying more is usually the move. But I get it.

b.emotion: not convinced, but letting it go

options.text: "Terrifying, honestly. But I'm glad I owned up to it."
"It's fine now, no big deal." (downplay it)

a.text: Deadass? In front of the whole class? That's lowkey the bravest thing I've heard all week.

b.text: You keep saying 'no big deal' with a face that says extremely big deal.

options.text: Admit quietly that you're worried the truth will still come out.
Insist everything's fine, to Sam and to yourself.

a.text: ...Okay, that's not a 'fine' voice. That's a whole other voice.

b.emotion: not buying it, letting it drop anyway

lines.text: We built the whole thing from a scribble in Priya's notebook. Two weeks of arguing over glue and grading rubrics — and it's actually ours.
For the record — the rotating base was worth the extra three days.
You know you're the only one who actually asked, right? Not assumed. Asked.
Figured it was worth a shot.
It was more than a shot. It was, like — the thing.
I wasn't sure you'd actually back me up, that night at my place.
You were right. Wasn't hard once you said it out loud.
It's usually not, once someone says it.
The project was ours. Real, built-from-scratch ours. I just didn't say so when it mattered, and now that feels like its own small unfinished thing.
We rebuilt the whole thing in one night and you still let him mumble through it alone up there.
...Yeah. I know. I should've jumped in.
Two weeks of looking over my shoulder, and it turns out the scary part was just saying it out loud. Once.
I've never wanted to hug someone for getting us in trouble before.
I stopped bringing up ideas for a week because I thought you'd already decided I didn't matter to this.
I'm sorry. That was on me, not just the project.
...Redo starts tomorrow. We'll see.
lines.emotion: not quite forgiveness yet, but a door left open

lines.text: The grade's fine. Everything's fine. So why does 'fine' keep feeling like a countdown?
You told me to keep it between us. I did. Look how that's going.
lines.action: doesn't slow down as she says it

lines.text: Priya, wait—

voiceNotes: Theo (player) leans on 'honestly' right before admitting something he's nervous about, and turns statements into questions when he's unsure of himself — this should show up most in scenes where he's choosing between comfort and confrontation. Marcus performs even when it's just three people in a library — pauses for laughs that don't always come — and drops into flat monosyllables the moment someone corners him with something real ('...Um.' / '...Oh.'). Priya's tell is control: complete, ordered sentences when she's fine, clipped one-word answers when she isn't ('It's fine.' delivered exactly the way that means it isn't). Her silence in scene-5-silenced and scene-5-blamed should read as louder than her usual precision. Ms. Alvarez never raises her voice — the read is that her quiet is the consequence, not a lecture. Sam is the tonal release valve after heavier scenes; keep their lines slightly oblivious to emotional weight but not unkind, and let them catch on a beat late ('Wait— you good?') rather than immediately, which keeps them endearing instead of insightful.

alternatives: Honestly? This is moving kind of fast, right?
...Anyone else feel like we're speedrunning this?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Theo's trailing-off, question-tagged uncertainty but offers a slightly lighter/more deflective version if the tone needs to stay less heavy this early in the shortcut branch.

alternatives: Things have been kind of a lot at home lately. I didn't know how to bring it up without making it a whole thing.
Home's been rough. I didn't want it to turn into some big group therapy session, so I just... didn't say anything.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Marcus's admission needs to land as a genuine crack in the performance — alternatives preserve the monosyllabic buildup but vary how much vulnerability he allows himself in the actual phrasing.
Priya's line needs to sting through politeness rather than open hostility — 'Nice presentation' keeps her characteristic control while the subtext (this is a closed door) does the emotional work.
